data.title: Daqo New Energy CFO sees turning point for embattled solar industry
data.description:
China's solar industry has "marked a clear bottom, and it is already recovering" after recent meetings with the government to address severe overcapacity, Daqo New Energy (NYSE:DQ) CFO Ming Yang told Bloomberg in an interview Thursday.

Daqo (NYSE:DQ) reported a net loss of $76.5M in Q2, but Ming said the polysilicon sector's recovery should drive "significantly" improved earnings starting in Q3, and "within six months, before the end of the year, we should start to generate profitability."

Major Chinese solar companies plan to establish a fund of at least 50B yuan (~$7B) this quarter to purchase and shut down more than 1M tons of capacity, a plan that Ming said is being discussed and moving forward with the support of the government, with details remaining including how to fund the initiative and at what price capacity marked for closure will be purchased.

Solar-grade polysilicon, a key material in panels, is currently priced ~50 yuan/kg, and Ming said a level of 50-60 yuan/kg is reasonable, given current production levels and costs.

ETF: (NYSEARCA:TAN)

data.title: G-III Apparel signals $3.02B fiscal 2026 net sales target as company prioritizes owned brands amid tariff impact
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: G-III Apparel Group (GIII) Q2 2026

Management View

  • Morris Goldfarb, Chairman & CEO, opened by stating, "In the second quarter, we exceeded our expectations across both net sales and earnings. Net sales benefited from retailers responding to consumer demand for newness and fashion as we transition season." Goldfarb highlighted sales momentum from owned brands DKNY, Donna Karan, Karl Lagerfeld, and Vilebrequin, while gross margins were impacted by higher-than-expected tariff costs. He indicated active mitigation strategies, including vendor participation, selective sourcing shift, and targeted price increases, with the company absorbing some costs to remain competitive and capture market share.
  • Goldfarb emphasized strategic initiatives: "We're consolidating our warehouse network, exiting 4 facilities and reducing associated staff by year-end, which is expected to generate significant savings." The company is investing in technology, including 3D design and AI automation, to drive efficiencies and support product creation, speed to market, and consumer engagement strategies.
  • Brand performance was a focal point. Goldfarb reported, "Donna Karan has tapped into a white space opportunity...In the second quarter, the brand delivered strong results across its lifestyle offering led by continuous strength in dresses." He also noted, "Karl Lagerfeld is building momentum globally, delivering another quarter of strong growth led by North America."
  • On retail and digital presence, Goldfarb said, "Our owned websites delivered strong double-digit growth this quarter underscoring the significant value and long-term potential of the channel."
  • Goldfarb concluded, "We now expect net sales of approximately $3.02 billion, and non-GAAP diluted earnings per share between $2.55 and $2.75."
  • Neal Nackman, CFO, stated, "Net sales for the second quarter ended July 31, 2025, were $613 million, compared to $645 million in the same period last year, well ahead of our expectations, driven by our Wholesale segment." Nackman explained the decrease was primarily due to the exit from Calvin Klein jeans and Sportswear License businesses, with retail segment showing growth despite a smaller store footprint. He highlighted, "We remain in a strong financial position ending the quarter in a net cash position of $286 million after repurchasing $25 million in shares this past quarter."

Outlook

  • Goldfarb stated, "We now expect net sales of approximately $3.02 billion, and non-GAAP diluted earnings per share between $2.55 and $2.75." Nackman added, "We expect the total incremental cost of tariffs to be approximately $155 million, up from the $135 million original estimate." The updated outlook reflects a more cautious retail environment, the impact of tariffs, and the transition from PVH licenses, with owned brands projected to grow at a mid-single-digit rate this year.

Financial Results

  • Nackman reported, "Net sales for the second quarter ended July 31, 2025, were $613 million, compared to $645 million in the same period last year." Gross margin percentage was 40.8%, compared to 42.8% in the prior year's second quarter. Non-GAAP net income for the quarter was $11 million, or $0.25 per diluted share, compared to $24 million, or $0.52 per diluted share, in the previous year. Inventory was $640 million at quarter end, up 5% from last year, reflecting planned acceleration of inventory receipts due to tariffs. The company ended the quarter in a net cash position of $286 million after $25 million in share repurchases.

Q&A

  • Ashley Owens, KeyBanc: Asked about gross margin pressures, price elasticity, and the outlook for further margin pressure next year. Goldfarb responded, "We're targeting areas of our business where it's appropriate and acceptable to raise prices... The consumer has accepted some price increases that we've implemented at the tail end of Q2."
  • Owens followed up on the mix shift from PVH. Nackman replied, "No dramatic change in terms of percentage, pretty similar to where we've been before."
  • Mauricio Serna Vega, UBS: Sought detail on sales update and drivers of lower revenues from PVH brands vs. go-forward business. Nackman explained, "We've actually got challenges this year, both -- first and foremost, really from the transition of the businesses... I think when you combine that with the tariff pressures... it's nearly a perfect storm."
  • Serna Vega followed up on tariff impact. Nackman shared, "If I were to look at our reduction of just over 200 basis points, I would say it was probably half tariffs and half product mix."
  • Paul Kearney, Barclays: Inquired about the product from India and sales impact. Goldfarb said, "The amount of product we bring in from India historically has not been very much... I'm happy to give you the topline FOB number. It happens to be somewhere near $30 million, which does affect our fourth quarter, our year-end topline results should we have to abandon it."
  • Dana Telsey, Telsey Advisory Group: Asked about the performance and outlook for owned brands and new licenses. Goldfarb reported strong retailing and door expansion for key owned brands, and highlighted growth opportunities for Converse and BCBG licenses.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was generally probing, focusing on gross margin pressures, mitigation strategies, and the impact of tariffs and the PVH transition. The sentiment was neutral to slightly negative, with concerns about margin sustainability, pricing power, and potential sales headwinds.
  • Management maintained a confident tone in prepared remarks, with Goldfarb frequently stressing growth potential and brand strength. During Q&A, both Goldfarb and Nackman acknowledged challenges but remained optimistic. Goldfarb stated, "We believe it can be a $1 billion brand in the coming years," reflecting confidence in Donna Karan's growth.
  • Compared to the prior quarter, analyst questions shifted more toward the specifics of tariff impact and the loss of PVH licenses, while management's tone remained confident but more focused on risk mitigation.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance was revised down from $3.14 billion in Q1 to $3.02 billion in Q2, reflecting cautious retailer outlook and higher tariff impact.
  • Management previously withdrew net income and EBITDA guidance in Q1 due to uncertainty, but reinstated it in Q2.
  • The focus shifted from double-digit owned brand growth in Q1 to mid-single-digit growth expectations in Q2.
  • Analyst focus moved from general pricing power and inventory planning to more detailed questions about mitigation strategies and the magnitude of specific impacts from tariffs and PVH brand transitions.
  • Management's confidence in owned brands remained strong, but the language around sales and margin guidance became more cautious.

Risks and Concerns

  • Higher-than-expected tariff costs, with incremental tariffs now estimated at $155 million for the year.
  • Retail partners are "increasingly cautious on their inventory buys, in anticipation of tariff increases becoming more pronounced."
  • Disproportionate reduction in open to buy, particularly affecting Calvin Klein and Tommy Hilfiger businesses.
  • Transition risks from exiting major PVH licenses, with remaining PVH sales expected to drop significantly in fiscal 2027.
  • Product from India, representing around $30 million in sales, may have to be written off or redirected if tariffs persist.
  • Management is mitigating risks through vendor negotiations, price increases, sourcing shifts, and focused investment in owned brands.

Final Takeaway

G-III Apparel Group delivered second quarter results that surpassed internal expectations, driven by momentum in owned brands and disciplined cost controls amid significant tariff headwinds and cautious retail partner behavior. The company revised its fiscal 2026 net sales outlook to $3.02 billion and expects non-GAAP EPS between $2.55 and $2.75, reflecting the impact of tariffs and the transition away from PVH licenses. Management remains confident in the growth potential of its owned brands and is investing in technology, marketing, and supply chain optimization to drive future profitability, while maintaining a strong balance sheet to support strategic initiatives and shareholder returns.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Brady outlines $4.85–$5.15 adjusted EPS target for 2026 through cost actions and R&D focus
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Brady Corporation (BRC) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Russell Shaller, President and CEO, highlighted a record high adjusted EPS for both the quarter and fiscal year, noting "We grew organic sales 2.4% in the quarter. Acquisitions added 11.3% to our sales growth and we grew adjusted earnings per share by 5.9% to a new quarterly record of $1.26 per share." He emphasized strong performance in the Americas and Asia with 4.3% organic sales growth, contrasted by a 1.3% decline in Europe and Australia, though operating income in those regions improved after cost actions.
  • Shaller stated, "We increased R&D by 31% in the fourth quarter this year," driven by both organic investment and the acquisition of Gravotech and Funai's Microfluidics Solutions product line. He noted, "Printers and the encoded consumable products for our printers represent just under 40% of our sales in fiscal 2025."
  • Product innovation was a focus, with new launches like the i7500 industrial label printer outperforming targets and the recent acquisition of Mecco expanding direct part marking capabilities. Shaller added, "We are beginning to see the integration of multiple technologies into a single platform...to provide a more cost-effective solution to our customers."
  • The company returned $96 million to shareholders in fiscal 2025 and announced its 40th consecutive year of dividend increases.
  • Ann Thornton, CFO, stated, "Organic sales grew 2.4%, and we reported another quarterly record for adjusted earnings per share of $1.26 per share, which was up 5.9% from the fourth quarter of last year."

Outlook

  • Brady projects GAAP EPS of $4.55 to $4.85 per share for fiscal 2026 and adjusted EPS of $4.85 to $5.15 per share. Management said this represents "an increase of between 5.4% and 12% compared to fiscal '25."
  • The company anticipates "organic sales growth in the low single-digit percentages for the year ending July 31, 2026."
  • Other elements of guidance include an income tax rate of approximately 21%, depreciation and amortization expense of approximately $42 million, and capital expenditures of approximately $40 million.
  • Additional tariff expenses are estimated at $8 million to $12 million for 2026, with management implementing mitigating strategies.

Financial Results

  • Fourth quarter organic sales grew 2.4%, with acquisitions contributing 11.3% and foreign currency translation adding 2% for total sales growth of 15.7%.
  • Gross profit margin was reported at 50.4%. Excluding $1.9 million in cost of goods sold related to restructuring, gross margin would have been 50.9%.
  • SG&A expense was $117.9 million, representing 29.7% of sales. Excluding restructuring and amortization, SG&A was 26.8% of sales.
  • R&D spending set a new record at $23.1 million, or 5.8% of sales in the quarter.
  • Adjusted pretax earnings increased 5.1% to $74.2 million. Adjusted net income was $60.2 million, and adjusted diluted EPS reached $1.26 per share.
  • Operating cash flow for the quarter was $58.3 million, and free cash flow was $49.4 million.
  • Brady ended the quarter with a net cash position of $74.6 million and repurchased 257,000 shares for $17.7 million in Q4.

Q&A

  • Steve Ferazani, Sidoti: Asked about the "surprisingly high" EPS guidance in a challenging environment and how margin expansion will be achieved. Shaller responded, "We really took a decent amount of costs out of our structure in the last 2 quarters. And that in and of itself is going to get us a lot of the way there," and cited tariff mitigation strategies and price increases.
  • Ferazani queried the drop in free cash flow in Q4. Thornton explained, "The primary item that drove our cash flow down in Q4...was a little bit of inventory build really that happens throughout this fiscal year as we moved a couple of ... relatively large facilities."
  • Ferazani asked about R&D spend sustainability and acquisition impacts. Shaller said Funai's platform is "just a pure R&D platform" while Mecco "should be additive almost immediately."
  • Keith Housum, Northcoast Research: Raised the spike in R&D and whether spending would normalize. Shaller replied, "In the short term, it probably will come down a hair as we look to merge some of the R&D teams...I think that trend [of increasing R&D] continuing."
  • Housum asked about the timing of tariff impact. Shaller indicated it would be "more to the first couple of quarters" and confirmed mitigation efforts are included in guidance.
  • Housum inquired about growth drivers in printers and consumables. Shaller identified data centers and aerospace and defense as "the two principal growth areas that we're seeing."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a neutral to slightly positive tone, focusing on the feasibility of margin expansion, sustainability of R&D investment, and cash flow trends, expressing appreciation for management's detailed responses.
  • Management displayed confidence in cost actions and product innovation, using phrases like "I feel pretty good about the range that we gave" and "I think we've digested the worst of it right now." During Q&A, management's tone was steady and detailed, providing context and rationale for guidance and cost strategies.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management remained constructive but more focused on the operational challenges and mitigation strategies for tariffs and costs.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for fiscal 2026 is more specific, with a higher adjusted EPS range and explicit mention of a 5.4% to 12% increase over fiscal 2025. The previous quarter's guidance was for adjusted EPS of $4.48 to $4.63 per share.
  • Strategic focus shifted to integrating recent acquisitions like Mecco and Funai's Microfluidics Solutions, with emphasis on cost control and R&D as growth drivers.
  • Analysts' questions this quarter centered on the sustainability of margin expansion and R&D levels, whereas the previous quarter included more queries on geographic and segment performance.
  • Management's tone remained confident, but with more elaboration on cost actions, tariff impacts, and supply chain adjustments.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited continued risks from tariffs and rapidly changing trade policies, estimating a potential additional impact of $8 million to $12 million in fiscal 2026.
  • The macro environment in Europe and Australia was described as "increasingly challenging," prompting facility closures and reorganization costs.
  • Additional risks to guidance include the potential strengthening of the US dollar, inflationary pressures, and a slowdown in economic activity.
  • Mitigation strategies include cost reduction, supply chain adjustments, price increases, and ongoing investment in product innovation.

Final Takeaway

Brady Corporation closed fiscal 2025 with record adjusted EPS and outlined an ambitious adjusted EPS target for 2026, underpinned by disciplined cost actions, ongoing R&D investment, and strategic acquisitions. The company remains focused on driving growth in high-margin engineered products, particularly in data centers and aerospace, while carefully navigating tariff uncertainties and macroeconomic headwinds. Management affirmed its commitment to shareholder returns, innovation, and operational efficiency as it enters the next fiscal year.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Medtronic succeeds in Hugo robotic-assisted surgery system study
data.description:
Medtronic (NYSE:MDT) shares ticked up after the company met key effectiveness and safety goals in a study evaluating its Hugo robotic-assisted surgery system for inguinal and ventral hernia repairs.

The study reported a surgical success rate of 100%, exceeding the targeted performance goal of 85%. 

Coming to safety endpoints, in the case of inguinal hernia repairs, analysis of 92 patients revealed a surgical site event rate of 0.0%, which is significantly lower than anticipated. 

For ventral hernia repairs, the surgical site event rate was recorded at 2.1%, also well below the pre-established performance goal of 30%.

The Medtronic Hugo robotic-assisted surgery system is currently available for commercial use in select regions.

The company's shares are up 1.6% today post the news.

data.title: Fandango and TikTok team up to sell movie tickets
data.description:
Thanks to a tie-up with Fandango (NASDAQ:CMCSA), TikTok users can now purchase theater tickets directly on the social media app.

Tickets can be purchased through TikTok Spotlight, an integration that was launched by the site last year to highlight new movies and TV shows. By selecting the “Get Tickets” option on the TikTok site, users can select viewing times and purchase seats for their favorite releases.

Users can buy tickets beginning September 4 on select theatrical titles, starting with Disney’s (DIS) upcoming Tron: Ares with a nationwide release on October 10.

The collaboration between the Chinese platform and the leading online movie ticketer was “a powerful step in meeting audiences in their daily digital lives, making it easier to turn the excitement of discovering a movie into the instant gratification of securing your tickets in advance, with no lines and no waiting,” said Fandango’s SVP of marketing and partnerships, Claire Ripsteen.

data.title: House lawmakers probe CVS over bill lobbying in Louisiana
data.description:
Two Republican members of the House of Representatives are looking into allegations that CVS Health (NYSE:CVS) used confidential patient information as part of a lobbying effort in Louisiana to stop the passage of a bill in the state that would prevent pharmacy benefit managers from also owning local pharmacies.

House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform Chairman James Comer (Ky.) and Subcommittee on Federal Law Enforcement Chairman Clay Higgins (La.) are pressing CVS CEO David Joyner to see if the company used a text message campaign that apparently contained patient information as well as warnings that the Louisiana legislation, House Bill 358, could shut down pharmacies and increase prescription costs. The messages were sent to CVS customers.

CVS operates 119 pharmacies in the state. The company also owns the PBM CVS Caremark.

"This apparent conflict of interest is problematic because CVS Caremark manages prescription drug plans for thousands of Louisianians and also contracts with the [Office of Group Benefits], the insurance provider for Louisiana state employees," the letter reads. "This text message campaign raises ethical and potential legal issues if indeed CVS Pharmacy used confidential patient information, obtained through a state contract, to lobby against H.B. 358.

"The inflammatory and misleading text messages—which included threats of pharmacy location closures, increased prescription costs, and loss of service providers—sought to encourage CVS Pharmacy customers to contact Louisiana lawmakers to oppose the bill."

Comer and Higgins have asked Joyner for documents and communications related to CVS' use of customers' personal information for use in political advocacy or lobbying. 

A request for comment to CVS has yet to be returned to Seeking Alpha.

data.title: Fed's soft-landing bid faces test in August jobs report, with revisions looming
data.description:
The big jobs report due Friday is expected to show a "fairly steady" unemployment rate, capped by U.S. President Donald Trump's immigration crackdown, even with cooling job gains in the face of a low hiring, low firing environment. 

On average, economists expect nonfarm payrolls of just 74K in August, slightly above the prior month's 73K, and see the jobless rate inching up to 4.3% from 4.2% in July.

The Federal Reserve will continue to keep a close eye on the unemployment rate, in particular, as Trump's immigration policies limit labor supply, said Glassdoor Economist Daniel Zhao. Healthcare payroll gains -- among one of the largest contributors to overall employment -- will be another indicator worth watching.

"If we start to see a slowdown in healthcare, that really does raise some red flag about the sustainability of our jobs growth streak that we're on when the rest of the economy is so sluggish," Zhao said in a virtual interview with Seeking Alpha.

The big tell will be revisions. As in previous months, large revisions to earlier payroll figures could reshape the picture and spell trouble for the U.S. central bank's soft-landing efforts. With a labor market already slowing and inflation risks tilted higher in the face of tariff uncertainty, "there is less margin for error," Zhao said.

Markets remain confident the Fed will start cutting rates when it meets later in mid-September, following the last month's weak jobs report and Chair Jerome Powell's dovish shift at Jackson Hole. "If the August jobs report shows hiring is still weak and slack in the job market is widening, it will probably clinch the case for a rate cut at the September decision," said Comerica Chief Economist Bill Adams.

St. Louis Fed President Alberto Musalem noted this week that recent economic data reinforced his view of downside risks to the labor market, and warned that benchmark revisions in the upcoming jobs report "may reveal an even lower pace of recent payroll growth." Fresh data out Thursday showed more signs of slowing in the labor market, with private sector employment rising less than expected in August and job cuts amounting to the highest year-to-date total since 2020.

May and June payrolls both experienced outsized revisions in last month's report, signaling that job creation in the U.S. was weaker than previously estimated, and "we shouldn't be surprised if we see large revisions for June and July as well" in Friday's report, Zhao said.

He added that with June's hiring so "close to the edge," a downward revision could easily turn those gains into losses.

Note the annual revision of the Bureau of Labor Statistics' payrolls benchmark is slated for release next week -- making it the last major jobs data before the Federal Open Market Committee gathers -- and it could reveal that the seasonally adjusted level of nonfarm payrolls for March 2025 was 475K-790K lower than reported, according to Wells Fargo economists. Evercore ISI expects the level to drop by ~625K.

Meanwhile, wage growth is expected to remain elevated, though it's not "sending warning signals as an inflationary factor," Zhao said, noting "it's fairly close to where it was before the pandemic." With most signals pointing to a cooling job market, he doubts the Fed will lean too heavily on wages to delay rate cuts.

Average hourly earnings are expected to rise 0.3% M/M in August, holding steady from July's pace, per the average economist estimate. Average weekly hours are also expected to stall at 34.3.

"The problem for the Fed would be if the labor shortage leads to higher wages, in which case the Fed could be actually forced to hike interest rates," said SA analyst Damir Tokic. "Thus, the focus is on wage growth," he contended.

SA Editor Liz Kiesche contributed to this story.

data.title: US SEC unveils agenda to revamp crypto policies
data.description:
The U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission on Thursday unveiled its agenda, which could see proposals to revamp regulations related to cryptocurrency and ease Wall Street rules.

The SEC outlined several initiatives covering cryptocurrency policies, which were last reviewed or updated by SEC Chair Paul Atkins on September 4, 2025. The agenda covers proposing rules related to the offer and sale of crypto assets to help clarify the regulatory framework.

“This regulatory agenda reflects that it is a new day at the Securities and Exchange Commission,” Atkins said, adding that the items on the agenda “represent the Commission's renewed focus on supporting innovation, capital formation, market efficiency, and investor protection.”

The SEC considers covering deregulatory rule proposals to reduce compliance burdens to simplify capital raising and investor access to private businesses. The SEC said it would consider amending existing rules to improve and modernize them, while addressing disclosure burdens.

“Importantly, the agenda reflects our withdrawal of a host of items from the last Administration that do not align with the goal that regulation should be smart, effective, and appropriately tailored within the confines of our statutory authority,” the SEC added.

If enacted, the new policies could be a win for the crypto industry, which has been actively pushing for clear rules to normalize its adoption with traditional finance.

Earlier in July, U.S. President Donald Trump’s crypto working group called on financial regulators to take action and draft new crypto-specific rules. It has also called on the SEC to allow digital assets to trade at the federal level.

Trump had formed the Working Group on Digital Asset Markets to establish a comprehensive regulatory regime for digital assets and make the U.S. the "crypto capital of the world."

data.title: NY Fed's Williams expects moving rates closer to neutral will be appropriate
data.description:
New York Fed President John Williams said on Thursday he expects that it will soon be appropriate to move the Federal Reserve's policy rate toward what's considered a neutral stance — that is, where interest rates neither stimulate nor drag on the economy.

"Looking ahead, if progress on our dual mandate goals continues as in my baseline forecast, I anticipate it will become appropriate to move interest rates toward a more neutral stance over time," he said in prepared text for his opening remarks at the Economic Club of New York.

Currently, the federal funds rate target range is 4.25%-4.50%. The continued gradual cooling in the labor market leads him to believe that monetary policy is being "modestly restrictive."

It's a delicate balance, he said, "On the one hand, we need to keep the labor market in balance to ensure that the effects of tariffs do not spill over into a longer-lasting broad increase in inflation. On the other hand, maintaining a stance of 'too restrictive policy for too long' could increase risks to our maximum employment mandate."

Assessing the rate of job growth is being complicated by recent sharp changes in labor supply, Williams said. "I therefore put greater weight on other indicators of the level of labor market conditions in assessing the strength of the labor market."

Assessing the price stability side of the Fed's mandate is also made more challenging by the tariffs imposed in recent month. "The realized aggregate effects of tariffs so far have not been as large as expected earlier in the year, but it’s still early days, and it will take time for them to come to be fully realized," he said. 

Overall, he expects tariffs to increase overall prices by a total of 1%-1.5%, with effects continuing through the first half of 2026.

Regarding the U.S. economy, he expects 2025 GDP growth to be 1.25%-1.50% and PCE inflation to rise to 3%-3.25% this year then decline to ~2.5% next year.  Williams expects the unemployment rate to gradually rise to ~4.5% next year.

Developing... check back for updates.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Mazda reports decline in August U.S. sales
data.description:
• Mazda North American Operations (OTCPK:MZDAY) (OTCPK:MZDAF) reported August U.S. sales of 38,140 vehicles, down 7.6% from a year earlier.
  • On a daily selling rate basis, sales declined 4.1%, with 27 selling days this year versus 28 in August 2024.
  • Sales rose 3.5% to 293,495 vehicles, YTD. 
  • Certified pre-owned [CPO] sales fell 4% in August to 7,212 units, with year-to-date CPO sales down 1% at 50,550.
  • The CX-90 posted record August sales of 6,801 units, while the CX-50 recorded its second-best August with 10,028 units sold.
  • Mazda Canada sales declined 2% year-over-year in August to 8,274 vehicles but rose 15.7% year-to-date. Sales in Mexico fell 6% to 7,927 units in August, with year-to-date sales up 10% at 67,639.
  • Source Press Release
